title: Fatigue, numbness, and imbalance with macrocytic cytopenias in an older woman

chart.oneLiner: 65F with type 2 diabetes on metformin, autoimmune hypothyroidism on levothyroxine, and known atrial fibrillation presents with months of fatigue, symmetric numbness/tingling in the feet, and worsening unsteadiness; labs show a macrocytic anemia with mild pancytopenia.

opening: I've been so tired for months, and my feet feel numb and tingly — I keep feeling unsteady when I walk, especially in the dark.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 65-year-old woman, tired and slightly unsteady, but a good historian. Worried she is 'getting old fast' and afraid of falling.

HPI: Several months of progressive fatigue plus symmetric numbness and tingling in BOTH feet, creeping up the legs. Balance is worsening — she stumbles in the dark and holds the wall on stairs. Some memory fuzziness and low mood. A sore, smooth, burning tongue for a few weeks. NO overt bleeding, NO black or bloody stools, NO weight loss, NO night sweats, NO bone pain, NO recurrent infections. She had one brief fainting-type spell and notices her heart 'flutters' at times (known atrial fibrillation).
DIET/AUTOIMMUNE (reveal only when asked about diet, supplements, or medical/autoimmune/family history): Eats very little meat — mostly vegetarian for many years, no B12 supplement. Has autoimmune (Hashimoto) thyroid disease. Her sister has type 1 diabetes and vitiligo (a personal/family autoimmune cluster, raising suspicion for pernicious anemia). She has had heartburn for years.
PMH: type 2 diabetes mellitus; autoimmune hypothyroidism (Hashimoto); atrial fibrillation; chronic GERD. NO known myelodysplasia, NO prior chemotherapy or radiation, NO heavy alcohol use.
MEDS (reveal on medication questioning): metformin 1000 mg twice daily for many YEARS (impairs B12 absorption); omeprazole daily for reflux (impairs B12 absorption); levothyroxine; metoprolol for rate control of atrial fibrillation; she is NOT on any anticoagulant (was 'never started'). NO methotrexate, NO trimethoprim, NO chemotherapy. ALLERGIES: none.
SOCIAL: retired teacher, lives alone in a two-story home with stairs, drives. Rare alcohol (a glass of wine on holidays). No tobacco, no drugs.
FAMILY: sister with type 1 diabetes and vitiligo; mother had a 'thyroid problem.'
ROS: as above; no fevers, no easy bruising beyond occasional, no hematuria, no melena, no dyspnea at rest.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: pale, mildly fatigued; subtle lemon-yellow tint to the skin/conjunctivae if jaundice is assessed (mild ineffective-erythropoiesis hemolysis), with pallor of the conjunctivae.
- Mouth: smooth, beefy-red tongue (atrophic glossitis) if the oropharynx is inspected.
- Vitals: BP 128/78, HR ~88 and IRREGULARLY IRREGULAR, RR 14, 36.8°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm with no discrete murmur appreciated (soft flow murmur only); no pericardial rub.
- Neuro: decreased VIBRATION and PROPRIOCEPTION in both feet; positive Romberg; reflexes variable (can be brisk with upgoing toes despite reduced ankle jerks — a mixed dorsal-column + corticospinal 'subacute combined degeneration' pattern); gait unsteady/wide-based.
- No lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly, no sternal tenderness, no petechiae/purpura on exam.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the vegetarian diet, the metformin/omeprazole, the autoimmune/family cluster, or the atrial fibrillation unless asked about diet/supplements, medications, medical/autoimmune/family history, or palpitations/heart rhythm.
- The dorsal-column/corticospinal neuro findings, glossitis, jaundice/pallor, and irregular rhythm are revealed ONLY if the relevant exam is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: Hgb 9.4, MCV 114 (markedly macrocytic), WBC 3.4 (low) with hypersegmented neutrophils, Plt 118 (low) — macrocytic anemia with MILD PANCYTOPENIA. Reticulocyte count low/inappropriately normal.
labs.Smear: Macro-ovalocytes and HYPERSEGMENTED neutrophils (>5 lobes); NO blasts, NO Auer rods, NO hypogranular or pseudo-Pelger-Huet cells (favors megaloblastic process over myelodysplasia).
labs.B12: Low serum B12 (well below the reference range).
labs.MMA_Homocysteine: Methylmalonic acid AND homocysteine BOTH markedly elevated — confirms true intracellular B12 deficiency and effectively excludes a spurious low B12.
labs.Folate: Serum and RBC folate NORMAL (excludes concurrent folate deficiency as the cause).
labs.Other: LDH high and indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin mildly elevated with low haptoglobin — ineffective intramedullary erythropoiesis mimicking hemolysis; TSH NORMAL on levothyroxine (treated hypothyroidism, not the current driver); INTRINSIC-FACTOR antibodies POSITIVE and anti-parietal-cell antibodies positive (pernicious anemia); iron studies normal. Glucose mildly elevated; renal function normal.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Atrial fibrillation: irregularly irregular RR intervals with absent P waves and a fibrillatory baseline; ventricular rate ~80–90; narrow QRS; no acute ischemic ST/T changes.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: atrial fibrillation with irregularly irregular RR intervals, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline, controlled ventricular rate, no acute ischemia. Self-host with appropriate credit.

ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular response
searchTerms: irregularly irregular no P waves controlled rate
ekg.clinicalVignette: An older woman with palpitations and an irregular pulse has a 12-lead obtained during her anemia workup.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Tortuous, unfolded thoracic aorta with calcification of the aortic knob; lungs clear; no effusion, mass, or cardiomegaly.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Tortuous, unfolded thoracic aorta with calcification of the aortic knob, clear lungs, no mass. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing tortuous calcified aorta with clear lungs
cxr.clinicalVignette: An older woman with longstanding hypertension has a screening chest film during her anemia workup.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, give your differential for this macrocytic anemia with mild pancytopenia (note your leading diagnosis). Also name THREE distinct etiologies/mechanisms by which a patient like this can develop your leading diagnosis.
steps.rubric: B12 (cobalamin) deficiency with neurologic involvement (leading,6). Myelodysplastic syndrome — must-not-miss for macrocytosis WITH cytopenias in an older adult (CANT-MISS,5). Folate deficiency (core,2). Hypothyroidism contribution (core,1). Drug/medication effect (metformin, alcohol) (core,1). Connects MACROCYTIC anemia + mild pancytopenia + dorsal-column/corticospinal signs to B12 deficiency rather than MDS (core,4). Names THREE mechanisms of B12 deficiency — e.g. pernicious anemia/autoimmune gastric atrophy, dietary inadequacy (low-meat/vegan), and malabsorption from drugs (metformin, PPI) or terminal-ileal disease (core,6 — 2 each). PENALTY: anchoring on B12 and never considering MDS in an older pancytopenic patient (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: B12 (cobalamin) deficiency with neurologic involvement (subacute combined degeneration). Must-not-miss alternative: Myelodysplastic syndrome — macrocytosis WITH a falling WBC and platelet count in a 65-year-old is exactly the picture that can be early MDS, and it must be actively excluded rather than assumed. Also reasonable: folate deficiency, an under-treated hypothyroid contribution, and drug/alcohol effect. Three distinct mechanisms of B12 deficiency here: (1) autoimmune pernicious anemia — gastric parietal-cell loss and intrinsic-factor antibodies (supported by her Hashimoto thyroiditis and family autoimmune cluster); (2) dietary inadequacy — years of a low-meat/vegetarian diet without supplementation; and (3) malabsorption from her chronic medications — metformin and a proton-pump inhibitor both impair B12 uptake (other causes in this class include terminal-ileal Crohn's, ileal resection, or fish-tapeworm).

coreActions.item: Connects macrocytic anemia + mild pancytopenia + dorsal-column/corticospinal signs to B12 deficiency
Names three mechanisms of B12 deficiency (pernicious/autoimmune, dietary, malabsorption — metformin/PPI/ileal)

penalties.item: Anchors on B12 and never considers MDS in an older pancytopenic patient

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order to resolve your leading diagnosis versus the must-not-miss alternative, and to characterize the other abnormalities?
steps.rubric: CBC with indices + peripheral smear, looking specifically for hypersegmented neutrophils vs blasts/dysplasia (CANT-MISS,4). Serum B12 (CANT-MISS,3). Methylmalonic acid and homocysteine to confirm true deficiency (core,2). Folate + RBC folate (core,1). Reticulocyte count and hemolysis labs — LDH, indirect bilirubin, haptoglobin (core,2). Intrinsic-factor / parietal-cell antibodies for pernicious anemia (core,1). TSH (core,1). EKG given her arrhythmia (core,1). PENALTY: jumping to bone-marrow biopsy before checking B12/MMA in a treatable megaloblastic picture (-2); not measuring B12 at all in a macrocytic anemia with neuro signs (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; CBC with indices and reticulocyte count; peripheral smear (look for hypersegmented neutrophils versus blasts/dysplastic forms to separate B12 deficiency from MDS); serum B12; methylmalonic acid and homocysteine to confirm true intracellular deficiency; serum and RBC folate; hemolysis labs (LDH, indirect bilirubin, haptoglobin) to explain ineffective erythropoiesis; intrinsic-factor and anti-parietal-cell antibodies for pernicious anemia; TSH; iron studies. Reserve bone-marrow biopsy for after B12/folate replacement if the cytopenias do not correct.

criticalActions.item: CBC with indices + peripheral smear (hypersegmented neutrophils vs blasts/dysplasia)

coreActions.item: Reticulocyte count and hemolysis labs (LDH, indirect bilirubin, haptoglobin)

penalties.item: Bone-marrow biopsy before checking B12/MMA in a treatable megaloblastic picture
not measuring B12 in a macrocytic anemia with neuro signs

requiredTests: CBC with indices + peripheral smear (hypersegmented neutrophils vs blasts/dysplasia)
contraindicatedTests: Bone-marrow biopsy before checking B12/MMA in a treatable megaloblastic picture
not measuring B12 in a macrocytic anemia with neuro signs

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is this patient's own 12-lead EKG, taken because her pulse was irregular. Describe your findings and their relevance to her management.
steps.rubric: Findings = atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response: irregularly irregular RR intervals, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline, narrow QRS, rate ~80–90, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: identifies atrial fibrillation — irregularly irregular rhythm with absent P waves (7); notes the rate is controlled and the QRS narrow with no acute ischemic ST/T changes (5); systematic read with pertinent negatives (3). PENALTY: calling it sinus/normal or missing the absent P waves and irregular rhythm (-3).
keyFindings: irregularly irregular rhythm with absent P waves (atrial fibrillation)
controlled ventricular rate, narrow QRS, no acute ischemia
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response.
commonMistakes: calling an irregularly irregular rhythm sinus arrhythmia or normal
missing the absent P waves / fibrillatory baseline
over-reading rate-related ST changes as ischemia
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation: irregularly irregular RR intervals with absent P waves and a fibrillatory baseline, ventricular rate ~80–90 (controlled), narrow QRS, and no acute ST/T changes. This is her known atrial fibrillation, and because she is NOT on anticoagulation it is directly relevant: an older woman with AF and diabetes/hypertension has an elevated CHA2DS2-VASc score and needs a stroke-prevention decision as part of management.

criticalActions: Atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response.
coreActions: irregularly irregular rhythm with absent P waves (atrial fibrillation)
controlled ventricular rate, narrow QRS, no acute ischemia

penalties.label: calls the study sinus/normal or misses the atrial fibrillation

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = atherosclerotic, tortuous thoracic aorta with a calcified aortic knob; otherwise clear lungs and no mass. CREDIT: identifies the tortuous/unfolded aorta with calcification (6); recognizes it as chronic atherosclerotic change and an incidental finding, not acute (4). PENALTY: calling the study normal, missing the aortic calcification, or mistaking the unfolded aorta for dissection/mediastinal widening (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes it as chronic atherosclerotic change
commonMistakes: mistaking an unfolded aorta for mediastinal widening from dissection
calling a film with clear lungs abnormal in the wrong way
steps.idealAnswer: Atherosclerotic, tortuous thoracic aorta with a calcified aortic knob; lungs are clear and there is no mass, effusion, or cardiomegaly. This is a chronic, incidental atherosclerotic change — not an acute process and not the cause of her anemia.

coreActions: recognizes it as chronic atherosclerotic change

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: You now have: macrocytic anemia with mild pancytopenia, LOW B12 with markedly elevated MMA and homocysteine, normal folate, positive intrinsic-factor antibodies, a smear with hypersegmented neutrophils and no blasts/dysplasia, and dorsal-column/corticospinal signs. Commit to a diagnosis and cause, state the single test that best discriminates B12 deficiency from myelodysplasia, and name any residual uncertainty.
steps.rubric: Commits to B12 (cobalamin) deficiency with subacute combined degeneration (6). Names the discriminator — elevated METHYLMALONIC ACID (with homocysteine) confirms true B12 deficiency, and the megaloblastic smear without blasts/dysplasia argues against MDS; states the cause as pernicious anemia (positive IF antibodies) compounded by diet and metformin/PPI (3). Acknowledges residual uncertainty — if the cytopenias do NOT correct with B12 replacement, a bone-marrow biopsy is needed to exclude coexisting MDS (1).
steps.idealAnswer: Commit to B12 (cobalamin) deficiency with subacute combined degeneration. The markedly elevated methylmalonic acid (with homocysteine) confirms a TRUE intracellular B12 deficiency, and the megaloblastic smear with hypersegmented neutrophils and NO blasts or dysplastic forms argues against myelodysplasia — so the discriminating test that resolves the fork is the elevated MMA/homocysteine plus the smear morphology. The cause is autoimmune pernicious anemia (positive intrinsic-factor and parietal-cell antibodies, fitting her Hashimoto thyroiditis and family autoimmune cluster), compounded by a long low-meat diet and years of metformin and a proton-pump inhibitor. Residual uncertainty: if the anemia and the mild pancytopenia do not correct after B12 (and folate) repletion with an appropriate reticulocyte response, a bone-marrow biopsy is warranted to exclude a coexisting myelodysplastic syndrome.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including treatment, the medication issues raised by her comorbidities, and her disposition/safe-discharge plan?
steps.rubric: Per MGH anemia work-up (B12): B12/cobalamin replacement, IM (or high-dose oral) given neurologic involvement (CANT-MISS,6); check folate and replete but do NOT give folate alone (can precipitate neurologic worsening) (CANT-MISS,4); identify and address the cause — pernicious anemia plus reconcile contributing meds (metformin, omeprazole) and counsel on diet/supplementation (4); monitor reticulocyte response and POTASSIUM as cells regenerate (3); address her atrial fibrillation seen on EKG — calculate CHA2DS2-VASc and start/refer for anticoagulation since she is currently untreated (3); arrange SAFE DISCHARGE — home-safety/fall-prevention and PT for her ataxia, medication reconciliation, and close primary-care/hematology follow-up to recheck CBC (3); neurologic follow-up for the SCD (2). PENALTY: giving folate without B12 in a combined picture (-3); discharging without addressing fall risk or the untreated atrial fibrillation (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Replace vitamin B12 (cobalamin) — IM loading (or high-dose oral) given her neurologic involvement, then maintenance. Check folate and replete it, but do NOT give folate alone — it can correct the anemia while the neurologic disease worsens. Treat the cause: this is pernicious anemia, so she will need lifelong replacement; also reconcile her medications — metformin and omeprazole both impair B12 absorption, so review whether the PPI is still needed and counsel on diet/supplementation. Monitor the reticulocyte response and watch POTASSIUM, which can drop as new cells are produced. Address the atrial fibrillation on her EKG: she is NOT anticoagulated, so calculate her CHA2DS2-VASc score (elevated by age, sex, diabetes, hypertension) and start or arrange anticoagulation for stroke prevention while continuing rate control. Arrange a SAFE DISCHARGE: home-safety/fall-prevention measures and physical therapy/gait assessment for her proprioceptive ataxia (especially relevant before starting an anticoagulant), full medication reconciliation, and close primary-care and hematology follow-up to recheck the CBC and confirm the cytopenias correct, plus neurology follow-up for the subacute combined degeneration. This can be managed as an outpatient.

criticalActions.item: B12 / cobalamin replacement (IM or high-dose oral, given neuro involvement)
Check and replete folate but do NOT give folate alone

coreActions.item: Identify cause (pernicious anemia) and reconcile contributing meds (metformin, PPI) + diet
Address atrial fibrillation: CHA2DS2-VASc and start/refer for anticoagulation
Arrange safe discharge: fall-prevention/PT, medication reconciliation, close follow-up
Neurology follow-up for subacute combined degeneration

bonusActions.item: Anti-IF / parietal-cell antibodies to confirm pernicious anemia

penalties.item: Discharging without addressing fall risk or the untreated atrial fibrillation

requiredActions: B12 / cobalamin replacement (IM or high-dose oral, given neuro involvement)

optionalActions: Identify cause (pernicious anemia) and reconcile contributing meds (metformin, PPI) + diet
Address atrial fibrillation: CHA2DS2-VASc and start/refer for anticoagulation
Arrange safe discharge: fall-prevention/PT, medication reconciliation, close follow-up

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 65F with type 2 diabetes on metformin, autoimmune hypothyroidism, atrial fibrillation, and chronic PPI use presents with months of fatigue, distal numbness/tingling, and gait imbalance, with labs showing a macrocytic anemia and mild pancytopenia. The diagnosis is B12 deficiency with subacute combined degeneration from autoimmune pernicious anemia, compounded by a low-meat diet and metformin/PPI malabsorption. It is reached by eliciting the focused history (diet, meds, autoimmune/family cluster, rhythm), performing the targeted neuro/cardiac/mucosal exam, confirming true deficiency with elevated MMA/homocysteine and a megaloblastic smear, and actively excluding myelodysplasia as the competing must-not-miss. Her own EKG shows the atrial fibrillation, which adds a stroke-prevention task to management.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Neuro: decreased VIBRATION and PROPRIOCEPTION in both feet; positive Romberg; reflexes variable; gait unsteady/wide-based — a subacute combined degeneration pattern.
Neuro: decreased VIBRATION and PROPRIOCEPTION in both feet; positive Romberg; reflexes variable; gait unsteady/wide-based — a subacute combined degeneration pattern.
Neuro: decreased VIBRATION and PROPRIOCEPTION in both feet; positive Romberg; reflexes variable; gait unsteady/wide-based — a subacute combined degeneration pattern.
Neuro: reflexes variable — brisk with upgoing (Babinski) toes despite reduced ankle jerks, a mixed dorsal-column + corticospinal subacute combined degeneration pattern.
Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm with no discrete murmur (soft flow murmur only); no rub — consistent with her atrial fibrillation.
General: pale conjunctivae with a subtle lemon-yellow tint to the skin/sclerae — mild jaundice from ineffective erythropoiesis.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals (note the irregular pulse).
Elicit the focused history, including diet/supplements, the full medication list, and the autoimmune/family cluster only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted neuro, cardiac, mucosal, and jaundice exam to reveal the subacute combined degeneration pattern and the irregular rhythm.
Build a differential led by B12 deficiency while explicitly excluding myelodysplasia, and name three mechanisms of B12 deficiency.
Order a focused workup that separates B12 deficiency from MDS (smear, MMA/homocysteine, IF antibodies) and interpret the patient's own EKG and the CXR.
Commit to B12 deficiency from pernicious anemia, then manage with B12 (not folate alone), reticulocyte/potassium monitoring, anticoagulation for AF, and a safe-discharge plan.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on B12 and never excluding myelodysplasia in an older pancytopenic patient
Discharging without addressing fall risk or the untreated atrial fibrillation
managementPearls: B12 / cobalamin replacement (IM or high-dose oral, given neuro involvement)
Check and replete folate but do NOT give folate alone
criticalWorkupItems: CBC with indices + peripheral smear (hypersegmented neutrophils vs blasts/dysplasia)
criticalInterpretationItems: Atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response.
criticalManagementItems: B12 / cobalamin replacement (IM or high-dose oral, given neuro involvement)
Check and replete folate but do NOT give folate alone
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response.
imageInterpretationPearls: An irregularly irregular rhythm with absent P waves is atrial fibrillation — name it, then act on stroke prevention.
The EKG here is the patient's OWN study; tie its finding to her anticoagulation decision.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's fatigue and fear of falling
Asks permission before sensitive questions (diet, alcohol)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
